title: Search Autocomplete — System Design
description: Designing search autocomplete (typeahead): as a user types a prefix, instantly suggest the most likely completions. The interesting problems are a read-heavy, latency-critical prefix lookup (every keystroke is a query), ranking suggestions by popularity, keeping suggestions fresh from the live query stream, and doing it for millions of QPS within tens of milliseconds. We build from a naive prefix scan toward a trie-backed, top-k-precomputed, cached, sharded suggestion service.

nodes.label: Popularity Aggregator
nodes.description: Popularity Aggregator: Asynchronous or background compute component that processes jobs, events, batches, or scheduled work.

nodes.label: Autocomplete API
nodes.description: Autocomplete API: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Index Builder
nodes.description: Index Builder: Background build pipeline that rebuilds the trie and top-k artifacts from fresh counts, then publishes versioned shard-local artifacts for atomic swap.

nodes.label: Edge / Prefix Cache
nodes.description: Edge / Prefix Cache: Caching layer that serves post-safety global suggestion responses keyed by prefix + locale/market + safe_mode + policy + index version, absorbing the skewed hot head of short prefixes.

nodes.label: CDN
nodes.description: CDN: Network-edge component used for global delivery, caching, or traffic acceleration.

nodes.label: Client / Search Box
nodes.description: Client / Search Box: Client-side application, SDK, portal, device, or upstream caller software outside the backend boundary.

nodes.label: Query Counts
nodes.description: Query Counts: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Event Collector
nodes.description: Event Collector: Synchronous application component that authenticates, normalizes, dedupes, rate-limits, and consent-checks executed-search events before they reach the stream.

nodes.label: Safety / Moderation Filter
nodes.description: Safety / Moderation Filter: Synchronous application component that screens suggestions against a block/restrict policy before they are returned.

nodes.label: Prefix Range Map
nodes.description: Prefix Range Map: Authoritative routing table mapping adaptive prefix ranges to shards and their live index-version artifacts.

nodes.label: Query / Term DB
nodes.description: Query / Term DB: Authoritative operational state store for structured records or correctness-critical state.

nodes.label: Personalization Service
nodes.description: Personalization Service: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: User History
nodes.description: User History: Authoritative or operational state store for records, metadata, counters, locks, or durable state.

nodes.label: Prefix Router
nodes.description: Prefix Router: Traffic boundary component that routes, balances, admits, or gates requests.

nodes.label: Hot Prefix Range Shard
nodes.description: Hot Prefix Range Shard: Derived lookup/top-k structure for an adaptive prefix range; hot ranges (e.g. 's', 'th') are split finer and replicated more heavily.

nodes.label: Long-Tail Range Shard
nodes.description: Long-Tail Range Shard: Derived lookup/top-k structure for a colder adaptive prefix range covering the long tail of less-common prefixes.

nodes.label: Spell / Fuzzy Matcher
nodes.description: Spell / Fuzzy Matcher: Synchronous application component that owns request-time business logic.

nodes.label: Query Event Stream
nodes.description: Query Event Stream: Append-only event stream, feed, bus, or log used for ordering, replay, or fan-out.

nodes.label: Precomputed Top-K per Prefix
nodes.description: Precomputed Top-K per Prefix: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

nodes.label: Prefix Index — Trie
nodes.description: Prefix Index — Trie: Derived lookup, search, ranking, or retrieval structure optimized for efficient reads.

functional: Given a prefix, return the top suggestions that start with it.
Reflect newly trending queries within minutes.
Optionally personalize suggestions to the user.
Respect locale/language: normalize and rank per market.
Filter unsafe/offensive suggestions and resist popularity manipulation.
nonFunctional: Very low latency: tens of milliseconds per keystroke.
Extremely read-heavy and high QPS (every keystroke queries).
Suggestions stay reasonably fresh as query popularity shifts.
Scale to a large vocabulary and global traffic.
Graceful degradation: return fewer/cached suggestions rather than fail.
Suggestions are eventually consistent — slight staleness is fine.
Privacy: personal history is consent-based, short-retention, and session/anon-scoped.
Abuse-resistant counting: deduped, rate-limited, low-trust events excluded.

capacity.label: Query rate
capacity.note: Each keystroke is a request; reads dominate everything.

capacity.label: Latency target
capacity.note: It must feel instant as the user types.

capacity.label: Vocabulary
capacity.note: The set of completable strings is huge and long-tailed.

capacity.label: Suggestions returned
capacity.note: Only the best few per prefix matter.

capacity.label: Freshness
capacity.note: Trending terms should appear soon, but not instantly.

capacity.label: Read:write
capacity.note: Index updates are batched; reads are constant and hot.

capacity.label: Prefix nodes
capacity.note: ~100M terms × ~15 chars avg, minus shared prefixes; node count, not k, dominates memory.

capacity.label: Top-k entry size
capacity.note: k is tiny, but multiplied by node count this is tens–hundreds of GB → must shard.

capacity.label: Index memory
capacity.note: Drives sharding by memory footprint as much as by QPS; use FST/DAWG sharing to shrink.

capacity.label: Cache hit rate
capacity.note: The skewed hot head of short prefixes means most QPS never reaches a shard.

capacity.label: Event ingest
capacity.note: Executed-search events feeding rolling counts; a fraction of read QPS.

capacity.label: Rebuild duration
capacity.note: Rebuilds are shard/range-local and parallelized, not one global artifact; the base rebuild bounds base-index freshness while a bounded real-time overlay covers viral terms in seconds between rebuilds. End-to-end freshness = input-count lag + build duration + artifact publish + canary hold + cache TTL adoption.

api.description: Return ranked completions for a prefix. The global, non-personalized response is aggressively edge-cacheable, keyed on prefix + locale + safe_mode + index version (the index version folds in the live tokenizer/normalization analyzer and moderation policy versions). `locale` (e.g. en-US) is the single market key — region and language are not separate dimensions. Personalization (when requested) is a re-rank at origin outside the edge cache so the global response stays shared; `anon_id` is sent only when `personalize=on`.

Record an executed search (feeds popularity). Posted to the event-collector API (not directly to the stream) so it can be authenticated, deduped, rate-limited, and consent-checked before counts move. The collector — not the client — computes canonical `normalized_query` server-side using the same locale analyzer as build/query time; the client never supplies normalized text (it is a ranking signal and must not be trusted from the client). The client may send an optional `analyzer_hint` (the client-side analyzer version it used for UX), but the collector always recomputes the canonical key. Idempotent on event_id.

dataModel.name: terms
dataModel.note: Completable strings with a popularity score.

dataModel.name: term_metadata
dataModel.note: Locale, language, and moderation/safety state for a term — consulted at build time and serve time. `locale` (e.g. en-US) is the canonical market key used by the index, counts, cache, and routing; `language`/`market`/`script` are descriptive attributes derived from it, not separate keying dimensions. Display text is kept separate from the normalized/indexed text.

fields.name: display_text
fields.type: varchar (rendered to the user, original form)

fields.name: normalized_text
fields.type: varchar (case/accent-folded, the key the trie/index is built on)

fields.name: normalization_version
fields.type: varchar (normalization ruleset version; change = versioned rebuild)

dataModel.name: prefix_topk
dataModel.note: Precomputed top-k completions per prefix — the read-optimized structure. Keyed by (prefix, locale) where locale (e.g. en-US) is the single market key — region and language are not separate dimensions. Entries carry score, rank reason, and the index version that produced them.

fields.name: locale
fields.type: varchar PK (full market key, e.g. en-US)

dataModel.name: index_versions
dataModel.note: One row per built index artifact — the unit of atomic swap, canary, and rollback. The version folds in the analyzer and policy inputs that shaped the artifact: a change to tokenizer_version, normalization_version, or moderation policy_version produces a new index_version. Because responses are keyed (cache + routing) on index_version, an analyzer or policy change naturally produces fresh cache entries and ages the old ones out.

fields.name: locale
fields.type: varchar (market key this artifact serves, e.g. en-US)

fields.name: tokenizer_version
fields.type: varchar (analyzer that produced the indexed keys)

fields.name: normalization_version
fields.type: varchar (normalization ruleset baked into this artifact)

fields.name: policy_version
fields.type: varchar (moderation policy applied at build time)

dataModel.name: prefix_range_map
dataModel.note: Routing control plane: maps each adaptive prefix range (scoped per locale/market) to the shard, its replicas, and the live index version serving it — read by the prefix router, updated by versioned rebuilds and rollback.

fields.name: locale
fields.type: varchar PK (market key the range is scoped to, e.g. en-US)

fields.name: prefix_range
fields.type: varchar PK (e.g. 'sa'–'sm'; hot ranges split finer)

dataModel.name: event_dedupe
dataModel.note: Dedupe ledger for the event collector: short-retention record of seen event_ids so replayed/duplicate executed-search events don't double-count popularity.

fields.name: event_id
fields.type: varchar PK (idempotency key from the event)

dataModel.name: moderation_decisions
dataModel.note: Audit/state behind term_metadata.safety_status: per-locale block/restrict decisions with policy version, reason, source, and reviewer — supports false-positive review and feedback loops.

dataModel.name: trending_overlay
dataModel.note: Bounded real-time overlay entries merged over the base top-k between full rebuilds, so viral brand-new terms surface in seconds; the next full rebuild absorbs and clears them.

fields.name: boost
fields.type: double (rank boost merged over base top-k)

fields.name: expires_at
fields.type: timestamptz (TTL until absorbed by next rebuild)

dataModel.name: query_counts
dataModel.note: Rolling, time-decayed popularity counts aggregated from the query event stream after dedupe and abuse filtering.

dataModel.name: user_history
dataModel.note: Optional per-user recent/started queries for personalization — consent-based, short-retention, session/anon-scoped.

fields.name: subject_id
fields.type: varchar (session/anon id, not a stable identity)

fields.name: consent
fields.type: boolean (opt-out drops the row & disables personalization)

patterns.name: Trie / prefix index
patterns.what: A tree keyed by characters so all completions of a prefix sit under one node.
patterns.whenToUse: Prefix lookups over a large vocabulary.

patterns.name: Precomputed top-k
patterns.what: Store the ranked best-k completions per prefix node so a query is a direct read, not a subtree scan.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy ranking where computing per query is too slow.

patterns.name: Edge caching
patterns.what: Cache popular prefixes near users (CDN/edge) since the same short prefixes are queried constantly.
patterns.whenToUse: High QPS with a heavily skewed prefix distribution.

patterns.name: Stream aggregation
patterns.what: Aggregate the live query stream into rolling popularity counts.
patterns.whenToUse: Deriving freshness/trends from a firehose of events.

patterns.name: Batch index rebuild (CQRS)
patterns.what: Rebuild the read-optimized index offline from counts, then swap it in atomically.
patterns.whenToUse: When the query structure is expensive to update in place.

patterns.name: Fuzzy matching
patterns.what: Tolerate typos via edit-distance or n-gram expansion of the prefix.
patterns.whenToUse: Real users mistype; exact prefix match alone misses intent.

patterns.name: Sharding by prefix
patterns.what: Partition the index by leading characters across nodes.
patterns.whenToUse: When the index or QPS outgrows one node.

steps.title: 1. Naive Prefix Scan (the baseline)
steps.description: Start with a table of terms and a LIKE 'prefix%' query at request time, sorted by popularity. It works for a small list; at 1M QPS over 100M terms with a tens-of-ms budget it's hopeless — which motivates a prefix index and precomputation.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why can't you run a 'LIKE prefix%' ORDER BY score query on every keystroke?

concepts.definition: Find all strings starting with a given prefix.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the core operation, run on literally every keystroke.

concepts.definition: Tens of ms end-to-end including network.
concepts.whyItMatters: Leaves almost no room for a scan-and-sort per request.

talkingPoints: Frame it as a read-heavy, latency-critical prefix problem.

traps.trap: Running LIKE 'prefix%' ORDER BY popularity per keystroke
traps.why: Scanning and ranking matching rows per request can't meet a tens-of-ms budget at 1M QPS.
traps.instead: Use a prefix index and precompute the top-k per prefix.

bottlenecks.issue: Per-request scan + sort over a huge term set.
A one-character prefix ('s') can match tens of millions of rows; even an indexed B-tree range scan must read the range and sort by score.
bottlenecks.mitigation: No index makes 'scan a huge range, then sort, in <50ms, ×1M QPS' feasible — the answer must be precomputed, not computed per request.

strong: Identifies read-heaviness and the latency budget as the drivers.
weak: Thinks a DB LIKE query is fine at scale.

whyNow: The baseline makes the trie and precomputation obvious responses.

view.caption: The client sends a prefix to the autocomplete API, which runs a LIKE 'prefix%' scan against the term database.

steps.title: 2. Trie Prefix Index
steps.description: Build a trie (prefix tree) keyed by characters: every completion of a prefix lives in the subtree under that prefix's node. Looking up a prefix becomes walking down the characters, turning a scan into an O(prefix length) traversal.
steps.decisionPrompt: How does a trie turn 'find all completions of a prefix' from a scan into a cheap lookup?

concepts.definition: A tree where each edge is a character and each path spells a prefix.
concepts.whyItMatters: All completions of a prefix share a subtree, so lookup is a short walk.

concepts.definition: Everything under a prefix node completes that prefix.
concepts.whyItMatters: Reduces 'which terms match' to a tree position.

options.name: Trie in memory (default)
options.description: Completions are stored in an in-memory trie keyed by characters, so resolving a prefix is just walking its nodes down to the matching subtree.
pros: O(prefix length) lookup; compact with shared prefixes.
In-memory => microsecond traversal.
cons: Still must rank the subtree's completions (addressed by top-k).
Rebuild/update strategy needed.

view.caption: The autocomplete API resolves a prefix by traversing the in-memory trie down to its matching subtree.

options.name: Sorted list + binary search on prefix
options.description: Terms are kept in a sorted array, so a prefix maps to a contiguous range found by binary search on the array boundaries.
pros: Dead simple; a prefix range is contiguous when sorted.
cons: Range can be huge for short prefixes; still must scan+rank the range.
Updates shift the array.

view.caption: The autocomplete API binary-searches the sorted term database to find the contiguous range matching the prefix.

deepDives.title: Trie implementations in production
points: A naive character-node trie is the teaching model, not the only implementation.
Production systems use compressed tries (radix/PATRICIA), FSTs, or DAWGs to share suffixes and shrink memory.
Lucene-style completion suggesters store the FST with weights so prefix lookup and ranking happen together.
All of these preserve the O(prefix length) lookup while cutting the node count that dominates memory.

deepDives.title: Locale-aware normalization & analyzers
points: A trie is keyed by normalized text, not raw input: 'Café', 'cafe', and 'CAFÉ' must collapse to one prefix path, so case-folding, accent/diacritic folding, and Unicode normalization (NFC/NFKC) run before indexing and at query time with the same analyzer.

'Prefix' is not universal: languages with no whitespace (Japanese, Chinese, Thai) need segmentation, German compounds need decompounding, and CJK/transliteration (romaji, pinyin) means a Latin-keystroke prefix may need to map to a non-Latin display form.
Keep display text separate from the indexed/normalized text and from the matched-key text — you index and match on normalized text but render the original display form.

Each index is per locale/market: 'system' ranks differently in en-US vs en-GB, and analyzers (tokenizer, stemmer, synonyms) are chosen per language. Pin a tokenizer_version / normalization_version so a re-analysis is a deliberate, versioned rebuild — not a silent drift between what was indexed and what a query normalizes to.
Because the analyzer affects the key, locale/market is part of the cache key and the prefix-range routing, not an afterthought on the response.

traps.trap: Walking the entire subtree to gather completions per query
traps.why: A short prefix like 's' has millions of completions — gathering them all per keystroke is slow.
traps.instead: Precompute and store the top-k at each node (next step), so lookup returns immediately.

traps.trap: Storing the trie only on disk
traps.why: Per-keystroke disk traversal blows the latency budget.
traps.instead: Keep the hot trie in memory; persist for rebuild.

failureDrills.scenario: A one-character prefix is queried heavily.
failureDrills.mitigation: Precomputed top-k at that node + caching make it a constant-time read.

strong: Chooses a trie and notes the subtree-ranking problem.

recap.after: A trie makes finding completions an O(prefix) walk.
recap.newRisk: Ranking the subtree's completions is still expensive — precompute it.

whyNow: The trie is the core index; top-k precomputation builds directly on it.

view.caption: The API walks the prefix trie, which an index builder constructs from terms in the query database.

steps.title: 3. Precomputed Top-K per Prefix
steps.description: At each trie node (prefix), precompute and store the ranked top-k completions by popularity. A query then walks to the node and returns its stored list directly — no subtree scan or per-query ranking. This is the key read optimization.
steps.decisionPrompt: A prefix has a million completions but you show 8. How do you avoid ranking a million per keystroke?

concepts.definition: Each prefix node caches its best-k completions, precomputed offline.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a query into a single read instead of a scan + sort.

concepts.definition: Expensive ranking happens at build time; queries are cheap reads.
concepts.whyItMatters: Matches the workload — billions of reads, batched writes.

deepDives.title: Building and storing top-k
points: Compute each node's top-k bottom-up: a node's candidates are its children's top-k plus its own terminal term.
Store the k suggestions inline at the node so a lookup is one read.
k is small (5–10) per node — but the number of prefix nodes (~10^8–10^9 for 100M terms) dominates storage, not k. Naive node-per-character memory runs to tens–hundreds of GB.
Shrink it with prefix sharing / FST / DAWG-style structures, and store top-k only for prefixes that are actually queried if memory is tight.
This memory footprint — not just QPS — is a primary reason the index is sharded later.

traps.why: Sorting a large candidate set per keystroke blows the latency budget at 1M QPS.
traps.instead: Precompute top-k per node offline; the query just reads it.

traps.trap: Storing full completion lists at every node
traps.why: Wastes memory and isn't needed — only the top few are shown.

failureDrills.scenario: Popularity shifts so the precomputed top-k is stale.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Suggestions are slightly behind, not wrong-shaped.
failureDrills.mitigation: Periodic rebuild from fresh counts (freshness step); staleness of minutes is acceptable.

strong: Precomputes top-k bottom-up and reads it directly.

recap.after: Each prefix node returns its precomputed top-k in one read.
recap.newRisk: Top-k goes stale as popularity shifts; it must be rebuilt periodically.

whyNow: Precomputed top-k is what makes the read path fast enough.

view.caption: The index builder ranks completions into the precomputed top-k attached to trie nodes, which the API reads after walking the prefix.

options.name: Precompute top-k per node offline (default)
options.description: An offline builder walks the trie bottom-up and stores, at each prefix node, the precomputed ranked top-k completions a query reads directly.
pros: A keystroke is one read of the node's stored list — no subtree scan or per-query ranking.
Matches the workload: ranking happens once at build time, billions of reads stay cheap.
Only the top 5–10 are stored per node, but total memory is controlled by compression, useful-prefix pruning, and sharding — not by k.
cons: Top-k goes stale as popularity shifts; needs periodic rebuild from fresh counts.
Build is a full bottom-up pass over the trie, expensive for a 100M-term vocabulary.
Brand-new trending terms can't appear until the next rebuild.

view.caption: An offline index builder stores ranked top-k completions on trie nodes, and the API walks the prefix to read them directly.

options.name: Rank the subtree at query time
options.description: No precomputation: each keystroke walks to the prefix node, gathers its whole subtree of completions, and ranks them by popularity on the fly.
pros: Always reflects the current popularity scores — no rebuild lag at all.
No extra top-k structure to store or keep in sync with the trie.
cons: A short prefix like 's' has millions of completions to gather and sort per keystroke — blows the tens-of-ms budget at 1M QPS.
Work scales with subtree size, exactly the wrong shape for an extremely read-heavy load.
CPU cost per request is unbounded for popular short prefixes.

view.caption: The API walks the trie to the prefix node, then gathers and ranks its whole subtree from the term database at query time.

options.name: Sorted-set leaderboard per prefix
options.description: Each prefix maps to a Redis sorted set of completions scored by popularity; a query reads the top-k with one ZRANGE and updates scores via ZADD.
pros: A prefix maps to a Redis sorted set; reading the top-k is a single ZRANGE.
Scores update incrementally (ZADD) so popularity stays current without a full rebuild.
Off-the-shelf primitive — no custom top-k build pipeline.
cons: One sorted set per prefix multiplies key count enormously across a 100M-term vocabulary.
Short prefixes still hold huge sets, so the hot keys are large and memory-heavy.
Per-prefix sets duplicate completions that a shared trie subtree would store once.
Write amplification: one executed query must ZADD into the leaderboard of every prefix of that query (not a single ZADD), so a popular term touches many keys per event.

view.caption: The API reads top-k from a per-prefix Redis sorted set via ZRANGE, which the index builder keeps scored with ZADD.

flows.name: Prefix lookup -> top-k read
flows.note: A keystroke walks the trie to the prefix node and returns its precomputed top-k directly — no subtree scan or ranking.

steps.title: 4. Edge Caching
steps.description: Prefix queries are wildly skewed — short, common prefixes dominate. Cache suggestion responses at the edge/CDN and in a prefix cache so the hottest prefixes never reach the index. Short TTLs keep them fresh enough.
steps.decisionPrompt: The same few thousand short prefixes are queried constantly. How do you avoid hitting the index for them?

concepts.definition: A small set of prefixes accounts for most traffic.
concepts.whyItMatters: Caching the hot head absorbs the vast majority of QPS.

concepts.definition: Cache responses near users with a short expiry.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cuts latency and load while bounding staleness.

traps.trap: Sending every keystroke to the index
traps.why: The hot head of prefixes hammers the index needlessly when their answer rarely changes within seconds.
traps.instead: Cache suggestions at the edge with a short TTL; only misses hit the index.

traps.why: Trending terms never appear because the cache never refreshes.
traps.instead: Short TTL (seconds–minutes) balances freshness against hit rate.

traps.trap: Caching the pre-safety response, or omitting safe_mode/policy from the cache key
traps.why: A response cached before safety screening (or under the wrong safe_mode / moderation policy version) can serve blocked or wrongly-filtered suggestions when the policy changes.
traps.instead: Only edge-cache post-safety global responses, key on safe_mode + index version (which folds in the moderation policy version), so a policy change yields a new key and ages old entries out.

failureDrills.mitigation: Edge cache serves the hot head; stale-while-revalidate covers a short outage.

strong: Exploits prefix skew with edge caching + short TTL.

recap.after: The hot head is served from cache; misses hit the precomputed index.
recap.newRisk: TTL trades freshness vs hit rate; cache must handle high fan-in.

whyNow: Caching is the cheapest way to absorb 1M QPS given the skew.

view.caption: Keystrokes hit the CDN, falling through to an edge/prefix cache on a miss, and only then to the API and trie.

options.name: Edge/CDN cache with short TTL (default)
options.description: Suggestion responses for hot prefixes are cached at the CDN edge with a short TTL, so most keystrokes are answered near the user before reaching the index.
pros: The skewed hot head of short prefixes is served near the user — most QPS never reaches the index.
Cuts latency to a CDN round-trip and shields the origin.
Short TTL plus stale-while-revalidate bounds staleness while surviving brief index outages.

Only post-safety global responses are edge-cacheable: the edge caches what the safety filter already screened, keyed on prefix + locale + safe_mode + index version (the index version folds in the live analyzer and moderation policy version), so a cache hit can return directly without re-screening.

Resolve the personalization conflict by design: the edge caches the global response, and personalization is a re-rank at origin for requests that opt in, so the cacheable path stays shared. For personalize=on, the cached global candidates are reused only as input to an origin-side re-rank followed by a safety screen — never returned straight from the edge.

Version discovery: the origin stamps the active index_version on each response (and can echo it as a versioned URL/header), so a swap to a new version — including an analyzer or moderation-policy change, which produces a new index_version — changes the cache key and old entries age out on TTL without an explicit purge; a short TTL plus stale-while-revalidate is the backstop while the edge learns the new active version.
cons: TTL trades freshness against hit rate — too long and trending terms lag.
Personalized responses can't be edge-cached (no user in the cache key), so personalize=on bypasses the edge and re-ranks at origin.
Cache key explosion if prefixes carry many query parameters — keep the key to prefix + locale + safe_mode + index version.

view.caption: The client's keystrokes hit the CDN edge cache, which falls through to the prefix cache, then the API and trie on a miss.

options.name: Shared app-tier cache only (no CDN)
options.description: A single shared cache in the app tier holds suggestion responses; every keystroke still reaches the origin region but hot prefixes are served from cache there.
pros: One central cache holds a consistent hot set and is simple to invalidate on rebuild.
Works for personalized or auth-scoped responses the CDN can't key on.
No CDN contract or edge-config complexity to manage.
cons: Every keystroke still crosses the network to your origin region — higher latency than the edge.
The shared cache becomes a high-fan-in hotspot at 1M QPS.
Loses the geographic locality that absorbs traffic closest to users.

view.caption: The client queries a single shared app-tier cache, which falls through to the API and trie only when the prefix misses.

options.name: Client-side prefix cache
options.description: The browser caches suggestion responses locally, so re-typing or backspacing a prefix the user just visited is answered from device memory with no network call.
pros: Re-typing or backspacing reuses results the browser already has — zero network for those.
Removes a slice of QPS from every layer behind it.
Instant response for prefixes the user just visited.
cons: Only helps a single user's own re-queries — no cross-user sharing of the hot head.
Can't reflect trending terms until the local entry expires.
Limited memory on device caps how much can be cached.

view.caption: The client serves repeated prefixes from its own local cache and only reaches the CDN, API, and trie on a miss.

flows.name: Edge cache hit vs miss
flows.note: The skewed hot head is served from the edge/CDN; only a miss falls through to the index.

steps.title: 5. Freshness from the Query Stream
steps.description: Keep suggestions current: executed searches flow into an event stream, an aggregator maintains rolling popularity counts, and the index is rebuilt (or incrementally updated) from those counts periodically and swapped in atomically.
steps.decisionPrompt: A new term suddenly trends. How does it start showing up in suggestions within minutes?

deepDives.title: Rebuild scope & freshness timing
points: The 'minutes per build' figure is a shard/range-local, parallelized rebuild — not one monolithic global trie/top-k artifact rebuilt and redistributed every few minutes. Each shard rebuilds its own prefix range from its slice of counts and publishes a versioned artifact independently.

Distinguish two freshness clocks: base-index freshness is bounded by the rebuild interval, while the bounded real-time overlay surfaces viral brand-new terms in seconds; the next base rebuild absorbs the overlay.

A build SLO is the sum of stages, not just build time: input-count lag (stream → counts) + build duration + artifact publish/rollout + canary hold window + edge-cache TTL adoption together define when a trending term actually reaches users.
Because builds are per-shard, a hot range can rebuild on a faster cadence than cold ranges, and a slow build on one range doesn't stall freshness everywhere.

deepDives.title: Observability for the freshness path
points: Stream lag (event-to-count delay) and dedupe/drop rate tell you whether counts are keeping up and how much traffic the collector is rejecting as duplicate or low-trust.
Rolling-count staleness and overlay size show how far behind the base index is and how much the real-time overlay is carrying.
Rebuild duration and live-index age bound freshness; alert when a build runs long or the live version stops advancing.
Canary failure rate gates swaps; track it alongside post-swap p99 and bad-suggestion reports so a build that passes the canary but degrades live is caught fast.

concepts.whyItMatters: They are the popularity signal that ranks suggestions and surfaces trends.

concepts.definition: Build a new top-k index offline, then switch reads to it in one step.
concepts.whyItMatters: Updates the read path without partial/inconsistent states.

options.name: Periodic batch rebuild + atomic swap (default)
options.description: A builder periodically rebuilds the trie and top-k lists from fresh popularity counts, then atomically swaps the new index in while reads stay on the old one.
pros: Simple, consistent; rebuild every few minutes from fresh counts.
Read path untouched during build, then swapped.
Each build is a versioned artifact: validate on a canary, then swap; roll back to the last-good version on a bad build.
After a swap the index version changes, so caches invalidate naturally by keying on it.
cons: Freshness limited to the rebuild interval; viral brand-new terms wait for the next build (covered by the overlay option).
Full rebuild cost for large vocab.
Swap, canary, and rollback must be coordinated per shard so the router never serves a mixed/missing version.

view.caption: The index builder periodically rebuilds the precomputed top-k lists and atomically swaps the fresh, versioned index in.

options.name: Base index + real-time trending overlay
options.description: Keep the periodic full rebuild as the base, but let the aggregator inject a small, bounded overlay of hot brand-new terms (or dirty-prefix patches) into the live top-k between builds, so viral terms appear within seconds.
pros: Viral brand-new terms surface in seconds without waiting for the next full rebuild.
The overlay is bounded (hot/dirty prefixes only), so it stays cheap and the base index keeps the read path simple.
The next full rebuild absorbs the overlay, so it never grows unbounded.
cons: Two paths to reason about: base + overlay must be merged consistently at read time.
Overlay writes touch the live index, so they need the same torn-read care as in-place updates (kept small bounds the risk).
Overlay ranking can disagree with the next rebuild until they reconcile.

view.caption: The builder periodically rebuilds the base top-k while the aggregator injects a bounded real-time overlay of hot/dirty prefixes for viral terms.

options.name: Incremental in-place updates
options.description: An aggregator updates the stored top-k lists in place as new popularity counts arrive, mutating the live index without a full rebuild or swap.
pros: Near-real-time freshness.
cons: Updating top-k lists in place under heavy reads is tricky and error-prone.
Risk of inconsistent reads mid-update.

view.caption: The popularity aggregator mutates the precomputed top-k lists in place as new counts arrive, with no full rebuild.

traps.trap: Updating the live index in place under 1M QPS reads
traps.why: Concurrent mutation of top-k lists risks inconsistent or torn reads on the hot path.
traps.instead: Rebuild offline and swap atomically; reads always see a complete version.

traps.trap: Counting raw query volume without decay
traps.why: Yesterday's blockbuster outranks today's trend forever.
traps.instead: Use a time-decayed/windowed count so popularity reflects recent behavior.

traps.trap: Counting events straight off the stream with no dedupe or trust check
traps.why: Query events are the ranking signal — a bot can replay events to push a term into top-k, and duplicate events double-count.
traps.instead: Ingest through a collector that dedupes by event_id, rate-limits sources, and excludes low-trust events before counts move.

traps.trap: Counting client-supplied normalized_query as the ranking key
traps.why: Normalized text is a ranking signal; if the client supplies it, a buggy or malicious client can distort counts, bypass the locale analyzer, or diverge from build/query-time normalization.
traps.instead: Have the collector recompute the canonical normalized key server-side with the same locale analyzer; treat any client-sent normalization as a UX hint only, never as a trusted count key.

failureDrills.scenario: The aggregator lags during a traffic spike.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Suggestions get a bit staler, never wrong-shaped.
failureDrills.mitigation: Reads keep serving the last good index; the rebuild catches up when the stream drains.

failureDrills.scenario: A rebuild produces a bad index (empty or corrupt top-k for some prefixes).
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reads never see the bad version; suggestions stay on the last good index.
failureDrills.mitigation: Validate the new version on a canary before swap; keep serving the last-good version, halt the cache purge for the new version, and replay counts into a fresh build after rollback.

failureDrills.scenario: A new index version passes the canary but causes a p99 latency spike or a surge of bad-suggestion reports once live.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The degradation is detected from live metrics and the range is rolled back to the previous version automatically, without operator firefighting.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat post-swap p99 by layer and bad-suggestion/no-result rate as release gates: roll the new version out per shard/range, watch live SLOs for a hold window, and auto-roll-back the range to its prior live_version if a guardrail trips. Widen the canary sample for the layers a small canary under-exercises (fuzzy fan-out, hot ranges).

strong: Aggregates the stream into decayed counts and rebuilds+swaps atomically.
Keeps mutation off the read path.
Versions each build, canaries it, and can roll back to the last-good index.
Names a bounded real-time overlay for viral terms between rebuilds.
weak: Mutates the live index in place.

recap.before: The index was static after build.
recap.after: Rolling counts from the query stream drive periodic atomic rebuilds.
recap.newRisk: Freshness is bounded by the rebuild interval; counts need decay.

whyNow: Without the stream feeding rebuilds, suggestions go stale and miss trends.

view.caption: Executed queries flow from clients through the event collector (dedupe, normalize, abuse-filter) into the stream, an aggregator updates decayed counts, and the builder rebuilds the top-k from them.

flows.name: Index update from the query stream
flows.note: Executed searches stream in, an aggregator updates decayed counts, and the builder rebuilds the top-k index and swaps it in atomically.

steps.title: 6. Typo Tolerance & Personalization
steps.description: Real users mistype and have individual intent. Add fuzzy matching (edit-distance / n-gram expansion) so 'recieve' still suggests 'receive', and optionally blend personalized suggestions from the user's own history with the global top-k.
steps.decisionPrompt: A user types 'systen' — how do you still suggest 'system design' without scanning everything?

concepts.definition: Match prefixes within a small number of character edits.
concepts.whyItMatters: Captures intent despite typos, which exact prefix match misses.

concepts.definition: Merge the user's history with global suggestions, weighted.
concepts.whyItMatters: Surfaces what this user likely means without ignoring global popularity.

deepDives.title: Fuzzy matching cheaply
points: Generate a few edit-distance-1 variants of the prefix and look each up — bounded extra work.
Or index n-grams so near-miss prefixes share keys; rank exact matches above fuzzy ones.
Blend personalization as a re-rank on top of global top-k, not a separate slow path.

deepDives.title: Ranking merge order
points: Exact-prefix matches rank first; fuzzy candidates fill in below them, never displacing a good exact match.
Personalized boosts apply last, as a capped re-rank — personal history can nudge order but can't erase globally useful suggestions.
Caps and a global fallback keep a user with thin/absent history from getting narrow or empty results.

traps.trap: Doing a global edit-distance search over the whole vocabulary
traps.why: Comparing the prefix to every term is far too slow for the latency budget.
traps.instead: Expand the prefix into a few bounded variants (or use n-gram keys) and look those up.

traps.trap: Replacing global suggestions entirely with personal history
traps.why: A user with little history then gets poor, narrow suggestions.
traps.instead: Blend/re-rank personal over global; fall back to global when history is thin.

traps.trap: Retaining personal search history indefinitely under a stable identity
traps.why: Autocomplete history is sensitive (health, finances, intent) and a privacy/retention liability if kept forever or tied to a durable id.
traps.instead: Make personalization consent-based, scope it to a session/anonymous id, set a short TTL, and honor opt-out by dropping the data and serving global suggestions.

traps.trap: Returning whatever ranked highest with no safety screen
traps.why: Suggestions show before the user even submits, so unsafe, defamatory, or private completions are surfaced proactively — a product risk, not optional polish.
traps.instead: Screen the merged suggestions through a safety/moderation filter (block/restrict policy) before returning, keyed by locale and safe_mode.

failureDrills.mitigation: Personalization is a re-rank layer over global top-k; absence degrades gracefully.

strong: Bounds fuzzy matching via prefix variants/n-grams.
Treats personalization as a re-rank with graceful fallback.
weak: Replaces global suggestions with sparse personal data.

recap.after: Typo-tolerant matching and optional personalization layered on.
recap.newRisk: Fuzzy and personalization add latency/complexity — keep them bounded and optional.

whyNow: Quality features come after the fast, fresh core works.

view.caption: The API fans out to a fuzzy matcher that expands candidate prefixes into the trie and to a personalization service that reads the user's history, then screens the merged suggestions through the safety filter before returning.

options.name: Bounded edit-distance prefix expansion (default)
options.description: Each typed prefix is expanded into a few edit-distance-1 variants, and every variant is looked up in the existing trie and merged with the exact-match results.
pros: Generate a few edit-distance-1 variants of the typed prefix and look each up — bounded extra work per keystroke.
Reuses the existing trie + top-k read path; no separate index to maintain.
Exact matches can be ranked above fuzzy ones so correct prefixes still win.
cons: Only catches small typos; two-edit errors slip through unless you widen the variant set.
Variant generation multiplies lookups, eating into the latency budget.
Transpositions and phonetic errors aren't well covered by raw edit distance.

view.caption: The API sends the prefix to the fuzzy matcher, which expands it into edit-distance variants and looks each up in the existing trie.

options.name: N-gram index for fuzzy match
options.description: A separate n-gram index maps overlapping character grams to terms, so near-miss prefixes are retrieved by shared grams and then re-ranked to drop false positives.
pros: Near-miss prefixes share n-gram keys, so candidates are retrieved by overlap rather than enumerated variants.
Handles transpositions and multiple edits more gracefully than fixed edit-distance-1.
Retrieval cost is roughly independent of the number of edits.
cons: A separate n-gram index to build, store, and keep in sync with the trie.
N-gram candidates need a re-rank/filter step to drop false positives.
Larger storage footprint than the prefix trie alone.

view.caption: The API's fuzzy matcher retrieves near-miss candidates from a separate n-gram index, which then resolves terms back through the trie.

options.name: Global edit-distance scan over the vocabulary
options.description: Every keystroke compares the typed prefix against the entire vocabulary by edit distance and keeps the closest terms, ignoring the trie entirely.
pros: Conceptually simple: compare the prefix against every term and keep the closest.
Catches any typo regardless of edit count or position.
cons: Comparing against 100M terms per keystroke is hopeless under a tens-of-ms budget.
Cost scales with vocabulary size, the exact opposite of what the workload needs.
Cannot reuse the cheap trie walk at all.

view.caption: The API's fuzzy matcher compares the prefix against every term in the database by edit distance, bypassing the trie entirely.

steps.title: 7. Scaling by Prefix Sharding
steps.description: When the index or QPS outgrows one node, shard by leading characters across nodes, fronted by a prefix router and the edge cache. Replicate shards for the read load. Most traffic is still absorbed by caching; sharding handles index size and misses.
steps.decisionPrompt: The trie no longer fits on one machine and misses still total huge QPS. How do you split it?

concepts.definition: Partition the index by leading character(s) across nodes.
concepts.whyItMatters: A query routes to exactly one shard by its prefix; no fan-out.

concepts.definition: Multiple copies of a shard to serve read load.
concepts.whyItMatters: Autocomplete is read-only at query time — replicate freely.

concepts.definition: A routing table of adaptive prefix ranges to shards (and their live index version), splitting hot ranges finer.
concepts.whyItMatters: Static alphabet splits skew; adaptive ranges balance load and coordinate versioned rebuilds.

options.name: Shard by leading characters + replicas (default)
options.description: The index is sharded by leading characters across nodes behind a prefix router, with read replicas per shard, so each prefix maps to exactly one shard.
pros: A prefix maps to one shard — no scatter-gather.
Read replicas scale QPS; the index need not fit one node.
A prefix range map lets hot ranges split finer and coordinates per-shard versioned rebuilds and rollback.
cons: Skew: some leading letters are far more common; needs adaptive ranges, not a static alphabet split.
Routing + rebuild coordination; fuzzy expansion needs bounded fan-out across ranges.

view.caption: The prefix router resolves each prefix against the range map to exactly one shard; hot ranges are split finer than a static alphabet cut.

options.name: Replicate the whole index to every node
options.description: The full index is replicated to every node, so any node can answer any prefix and the router needs no prefix-to-shard mapping.
pros: No routing; any node answers any prefix.
Simple.
cons: Every node must hold the entire index — caps vocabulary by one machine's memory.

view.caption: The prefix router forwards to a single node that holds the whole replicated index, so any node answers any prefix.

deepDives.title: Handling prefix skew
points: Letters/prefixes aren't uniform ('s','t' >> 'x','z') — balance by splitting hot ranges finer.
The edge cache absorbs the skewed hot head, so shards mostly serve the long tail.
Replicate hot shards more heavily than cold ones.

deepDives.title: Routing table & adaptive ranges
points: 'A–M / N–Z' is only the teaching diagram — real deployments use a Prefix Range Map of adaptive ranges, splitting hot ranges (e.g. 's', 'th') into finer shards.
The router resolves a prefix to its range, then to the shard plus the live index-version artifact serving it.
Exact prefix lookup routes to exactly one shard (no fan-out); fuzzy expansion can generate variants in other ranges, so cap it to a small bounded set of shards rather than scatter-gathering.

Per-shard rebuilds are versioned: the router only points a range at a new artifact after it's healthy, so traffic never hits a mixed/missing version, and it can roll back a range to its previous version.

deepDives.title: Operating it: SLOs & observability
points: Measure p50/p95/p99 per layer — CDN, API, router, shard lookup, fuzzy expansion, personalization, safety filter — not just end-to-end, so you know which layer ate the budget when p99 spikes.
Read-path health: CDN/edge hit rate, cache stale-serve rate, per-shard QPS, hot-range imbalance (shard skew), fuzzy fan-out count, and no-result rate.
Freshness health (from Step 5): stream lag, dedupe/drop rate, overlay size, rebuild duration, canary failure rate, and live-index age.
Safety/abuse health: safety block rate, false-positive review rate, low-trust event drop rate, and bad-suggestion report volume feeding the moderation loop.
Wire the release-gating signals (post-swap p99, bad-suggestion rate) to the range-map rollout so a bad version or rollout auto-rolls-back instead of paging.

traps.trap: Sharding by a hash of the full query
traps.why: Then completions of one prefix scatter across all shards, forcing scatter-gather per keystroke.
traps.instead: Shard by leading characters so a prefix maps to a single shard.

traps.why: The 's' shard melts while the 'z' shard idles.
traps.instead: Split hot prefix ranges finer and replicate them more.

traps.trap: Letting fuzzy expansion scatter-gather across all shards
traps.why: Edit-distance variants of a prefix can fall in other prefix ranges; querying every shard per keystroke reintroduces the fan-out sharding was meant to avoid.
traps.instead: Cap fuzzy expansion to a small, bounded set of candidate prefixes and route only to the few shards owning their ranges.

failureDrills.scenario: A new prefix range map is rolled out (e.g. splitting the hot 's' range) but the mapping is wrong — some prefixes point at a shard without that range's data, or two ranges overlap.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Blast radius is bounded to the changed ranges and the router rolls back to the prior range mapping; no shard ever serves a mixed or missing index version.
failureDrills.mitigation: Version the range map and roll it out per range with a hold window; the router resolves prefix → range → shard + live_version atomically, so a range only flips after its target shard reports the new version healthy. On a guardrail trip (no-result spike, errors on a range), revert just those ranges to the previous mapping/version while the rest keep serving.

strong: Shards by prefix (no fan-out), replicates reads, balances skew.
Lets the cache absorb the hot head.

recap.before: One node held the whole index and all misses.
recap.after: Prefix-sharded, replicated index behind the cache scales size and QPS.
recap.newRisk: Prefix skew needs active balancing; rebuilds must coordinate across shards.

whyNow: Sharding is the final lever once the fast, fresh, cached core is built.

view.caption: On a cache miss the API resolves the prefix against the range map via the router, which dispatches to the owning adaptive-range shard.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: keystrokes hit an edge/CDN cache that absorbs the skewed hot head (serving the global, non-personalized response); misses route through the prefix range map to a sharded, replicated in-memory trie whose nodes hold precomputed top-k completions; fuzzy match and optional personalization re-rank, and a safety filter screens the result before it returns. Executed searches go through an event collector (auth, dedupe, normalize, abuse-filter) into the stream, where a popularity aggregator maintains decayed counts that drive periodic versioned atomic rebuilds plus a bounded real-time overlay for viral terms.

view.caption: Client keystrokes pass through the CDN and edge cache to the API, which owns the request: it resolves the prefix range via the router to sharded tries with precomputed top-k (the router's only job is range lookup), then orchestrates bounded fuzzy expansion, optional personalization re-rank, and a safety screen. Executed queries flow through the event collector and stream into the aggregator and versioned index builder.

flows.name: End-to-end read: cache, route, fuzzy, personalize, safety
flows.note: A keystroke is answered at the edge if hot; on a miss it routes by prefix range to one shard, expands a bounded fuzzy set, optionally re-ranks with personalization, and is screened by safety before returning.

messages.label: exact prefix → resolve range map to owning shard
look up variants in few owning shards

functional.how: Trie walk to a node holding precomputed top-k.
Top-k built from decayed popularity counts.
Query stream -> rolling counts -> periodic rebuild.
Re-rank global top-k with user history.
Per-market normalization and a per-locale prefix_topk index; locale is part of the cache key.
Safety filter screens results before return; the event collector dedupes and excludes low-trust events from counts.

nonFunctional.how: Edge cache + in-memory trie + precomputed top-k = constant-time reads.
Edge caching absorbs the hot head; prefix shards + replicas serve misses.
Stream aggregation drives periodic atomic rebuilds.
Cache (stale-while-revalidate) serves during index outages; personalization is optional.
Atomic index swaps; bounded staleness is acceptable.
Consent-based, session/anon-scoped history with a short TTL and opt-out that falls back to global suggestions.
Event collector dedupes by event_id, rate-limits sources, and excludes low-trust events before counts move.

tradeoff: A custom trie/FST gives the smallest memory footprint and full control of the top-k build, but you own the index pipeline and ops.
Lucene/Elasticsearch suggesters are off-the-shelf and battle-tested, but heavier and less tunable for a 1M-QPS, tens-of-ms read path.
Redis sorted sets are simple but suffer key explosion and write amplification across a 100M-term vocabulary.
makesIrrelevant: A managed search service with built-in suggesters can remove the need to hand-build and shard a custom trie.

tradeoff: A managed CDN gives geographic locality and absorbs the skewed hot head near users; a self-managed app-tier cache is simpler to invalidate but loses edge locality.
Short TTL + stale-while-revalidate is the lever either way.
makesIrrelevant: A high edge hit rate (>90%) makes most shard-side read scaling unnecessary for the hot head.

tradeoff: Self-hosted Kafka/Pulsar gives retention and replay control for rebuilding counts, at the cost of running the cluster.
Managed streams remove ops but add per-event cost and quota limits at high ingest rates.

aws.name: Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink

tradeoff: Flink gives precise windowing and time-decay state for rolling popularity, but is operationally heavy.
Managed streaming aggregation trades that control for less tuning over watermarks and state size.

say: Restate it: instant top completions for a prefix, ranked by popularity, at huge QPS.
List functional (suggest, rank, freshness, typos) and non-functional (latency, QPS, freshness, eventual consistency).
Name the forces: read-heavy, latency-critical prefix lookup with skewed traffic.
Size QPS, latency budget, vocabulary, and how few suggestions you return.
Sketch GET /suggest (cacheable) and the query-event ingest.
Start naive to expose the latency problem, then a trie, then precomputed top-k.
Add edge caching for the skew, then stream-driven rebuilds for freshness.
Go deep where pushed: top-k build, freshness/rebuild, or fuzzy matching.
Shard by an adaptive prefix range map with replicas; let the cache absorb the hot head; balance skew.
Mention production guardrails: versioned rebuilds with rollback, a safety filter, and abuse-resistant event counting.
Summarize requirements and name the dominant tension (freshness vs read-path simplicity).

expectations: Reaches for a trie for prefix lookup instead of a DB scan.
Knows results should be ranked by popularity and reads dominate.
Precomputes top-k per node and caches the skewed hot head with TTLs.
Drives freshness via stream aggregation and atomic rebuilds.
Keeps all mutation off the 1M-QPS read path via offline rebuild + atomic swap.
Shards by prefix with replicas and handles letter-frequency skew.
Designs adaptive prefix-range-map rollout: per-range versioned rebuilds, hold windows, and automatic rollback gated on live SLOs.
Treats caching as policy-aware: only post-safety responses are cacheable, keyed by safe_mode + index version so analyzer/policy changes age entries out.
Runs ranking experiments (A/B of ranking changes) without disrupting the live read path.

followUps: How would you support multi-word / mid-phrase completion rather than just prefix-of-the-whole-string?

Internationalization: what tokenizer/analyzer and normalization strategy do you use for languages without whitespace (Japanese, Thai), compounds, transliteration, and multi-script input — and how do per-locale analyzers, indexes, and cache keys stay in sync across versioned rebuilds?

Safety already screens suggestions and the collector resists count manipulation — go deeper on the moderation workflow: policy versioning, automated vs human review, false-positive appeals, locale-specific policy, and the feedback loop from bad-suggestion reports back into block/restrict decisions.
How do you A/B test ranking changes without disrupting the live read path?
How would you support entity-aware suggestions (people, products) beyond raw query strings?
How do you handle a sudden viral spike for a brand-new term faster than the rebuild interval?

links.why: Useful for incremental indexing and freshness under large-scale updates.
